title: Combinational Circuits
summary: Combinational circuits implement Boolean functions using gate networks. Their output depends only on current inputs — there is no memory. This lesson covers adders, truth tables, and circuit design methodology.
content_html:
Combinational vs Sequential Circuits

CombinationalSequential
MemoryNoneHas state (flip-flops)
Output depends onCurrent inputs onlyInputs + past state
ExamplesGates, adders, MUXRegisters, counters, RAM

Half Adder

Adds two 1-bit inputs: Sum = X⊕Y, Carry = X·Y

XYSumCarry
0000
0110
1010
1101

Full Adder

Adds three bits (X, Y, Carry-in). Built from two half adders + one OR gate.

S = X⊕Y⊕Ci    Co = X·Y + Ci·(X⊕Y)

Programmable Gates

Using control bits, a single gate block can be programmed to perform different functions:

ExampleBehaviour
XOR as controlled inverter: C=0Output = A (buffer)
XOR as controlled inverter: C=1Output = NOT A (inverter)
AND as switch: A=0Output always 0 (gate closed)
AND as switch: A=1Output = B (gate open)
XOR as controlled inverter: When the control bit C=0, XOR passes A unchanged. When C=1, XOR inverts A. This is used in ALUs to select between addition and subtraction.

Multi-bit Addition

Chain full adders to add n-bit numbers. Use a half adder for bit 0, then full adders for bits 1 through n-1. Each carry-out feeds the next stage's carry-in.

A=69 (0x45) + B=12 (0x0C) = 81 (0x51)

Clock Synchronisation

Gates are not instantaneous — signals arrive at different times. A clock synchronises data flow by only sampling signals at fixed intervals:

  • Crystal oscillator, PLL, or 555 timer generates the clock
  • ALU needs signals to settle before reading the result
  • Modern CPUs run at 3-5 GHz clock speeds

exercises.answer: No memory — output depends only on current inputs
